text.title: Architecture Rules as Harmonize Tests
text.summary: Encode every layer / import / naming rule as a `@Test` case using Harmonize so violations surface as test failures, not lint warnings.

section.title: Tests, not READMEs
section.p: Harmonize (`perrystreetsoftware/Harmonize`) parses the project with SwiftSyntax and gives a fluent API over declarations: imports, types, protocols, paths. Each architecture rule lives as a `@Test` (Swift Testing) or `XCTestCase` (XCTest), runs in the standard test phase, and shows up in CI/IDE the same way as unit tests.

rule.statement: Every Swift module with internal layering MUST ship a `Tests/Architecture/` target containing at least one Harmonize test per layer boundary. CI MUST run `swift test --filter Architecture` (or include the target in the default scheme) on every PR; failures block merge. Agents are forbidden from suppressing a Harmonize test by adding the offending file to a Harmonize `excludedPaths` list — fix the import instead.
rule.rationale: Architecture in a README is invisible to an LLM iterating on a fix; an architecture test fails the agent's tool-use loop with a specific symbol and file, which is the only signal an autonomous agent reliably reacts to.

example.note: Each rule is one `@Test`; failure name pinpoints the violated invariant.
Without an executing rule, README intent is silently lost.

antipattern.description: Adding `Sources/Domain/User.swift` to Harmonize's `excludedPaths` to make the failing test green.
antipattern.reason: Excludes are meant for generated code; using them as an agent escape hatch turns the architecture suite into a no-op file-by-file.

section.reference: SPM manifest wiring Harmonize as a test dependency.
